name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
فَلَآ أُقْسِمُ بِمَا تُبْصِرُونَ (٣٨)
```

```english
I swear by what you see (38)
```

```arabic
وَمَا لَا تُبْصِرُونَ (٣٩)
```

```english
and what you do not see: (39)
```

```arabic
إِنَّهُۥ لَقَوْلُ رَسُولٍ كَرِيمٍ (٤٠)
```

```english
it is indeed the speech of a noble apostle (40)
```

```arabic
وَمَا هُوَ بِقَوْلِ شَاعِرٍ ۚ قَلِيلًا مَّا تُؤْمِنُونَ (٤١)
```

```english
and it is not the speech of a poet. Little is the faith that you have! (41)
```

```arabic
وَلَا بِقَوْلِ كَاهِنٍ ۚ قَلِيلًا مَّا تَذَكَّرُونَ (٤٢)
```

```english
Nor is it the speech of a soothsayer. Little is the admonition that you take! (42)
```

```arabic
تَنزِيلٌ مِّن رَّبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٤٣)
```

```english
Gradually sent down from the Lord of all the worlds. (43)
```

```arabic
وَلَوْ تَقَوَّلَ عَلَيْنَا بَعْضَ ٱلْأَقَاوِيلِ (٤٤)
```

```english
Had he faked any sayings in Our name, (44)
```

```arabic
لَأَخَذْنَا مِنْهُ بِٱلْيَمِينِ (٤٥)
```

```english
We would have surely seized him by the right hand (45)
```

```arabic
ثُمَّ لَقَطَعْنَا مِنْهُ ٱلْوَتِينَ (٤٦)
```

```english
and then cut off his aorta, (46)
```

```arabic
فَمَا مِنكُم مِّنْ أَحَدٍ عَنْهُ حَـٰجِزِينَ (٤٧)
```

```english
and none of you could have held Us off from him. (47)
```

```arabic
وَإِنَّهُۥ لَتَذْكِرَةٌ لِّلْمُتَّقِينَ (٤٨)
```

```english
Indeed it is a reminder for the Godwary. (48)
```

```arabic
وَإِنَّا لَنَعْلَمُ أَنَّ مِنكُم مُّكَذِّبِينَ (٤٩)
```

```english
Indeed We know that there are some among you who deny [it]. (49)
```

```arabic
وَإِنَّهُۥ لَحَسْرَةٌ عَلَى ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٥٠)
```

```english
And indeed it will be a [matter of] regret for the faithless. (50)
```

```arabic
وَإِنَّهُۥ لَحَقُّ ٱلْيَقِينِ (٥١)
```

```english
It is indeed certain truth. (51)
```

```arabic
فَسَبِّحْ بِٱسْمِ رَبِّكَ ٱلْعَظِيمِ (٥٢)
```

```english
So celebrate the Name of your Lord, the All-supreme. (52)
```

## (Commentary)

This is the third section of the verses of the Surah, reinforcing what preceded regarding the matter of the Inevitable Hour (الحاقة) through the tongue of confirming the Noble Quran, thereby establishing the truth of what it has foretold concerning the Resurrection (القيامة).

His saying, the Exalted: **فَلَا أُقْسِمُ بِمَا تُبْصِرُونَ وَمَا لَا تُبْصِرُونَ** "I swear by what you see, and what you do not see!" (69:38-39). The apparent meaning of the verse is an oath by what is witnessed by them and what they do not witness—that is, the world of the seen (الشهادة) and the Unseen (الغيب) — *that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge*. Thus, it is an oath by the totality of creation. It does not include His Transcendent Essence, for it is far removed from the etiquette of the Quran to join the Creator and the creation in a single rank, or for Him, the Exalted, to glorify Himself and His handiwork with a shared glorification in the same breath.

In an oath, there is a type of glorification and veneration for the object of the oath. His creation, the Exalted, insofar as it is His creation, is majestic and beautiful, because He, the Exalted, is Beautiful and nothing proceeds from Him except the beautiful. Indeed, He, the Exalted, has deemed His own act good and praised Himself for His creation in His saying: **الَّذِي أَحْسَنَ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ خَلَقَهُ** "who perfected everything He created" (32:7), and His saying: **فَتَبَارَكَ اللَّهُ أَحْسَنُ الْخَالِقِينَ** "Blessed is Allah, the best of creators" (23:14). Thus, existent beings possess nothing from Him, the Exalted, except beauty; whatever ugliness exists beyond that stems from their own selves or from the comparison of one to another.

In choosing "what they see and what they do not see" as the object of the oath to prove the truth of the Quran, there is an undeniable suitability. For the single, interconnected system (النظام) whose parts are intertwined and which runs through the totality of the universe necessitates His Oneness, the Exalted, and the return of all things to Him. This, in turn, necessitates the sending of messengers and the revelation of books, and the Quran is the best of heavenly books, guiding to the truth in all of that and to a straight path (الصراط المستقيم) — *the direct and unerring way to Allah*.

From what has preceded, the lack of soundness in the view that "what you see and what you do not see" refers to the creation and the Creator becomes clear, as the context does not support it. Similarly, the views that it refers to manifest and hidden blessings, or to the jinn, humans, and angels, or to bodies and souls, or to this world and the Hereafter, or to the observed effects of power and its unobserved secrets, are also weak; for the term is broader in its denotation (المدلول) than all of those.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّهُ لَقَوْلُ رَسُولٍ كَرِيمٍ** "it is indeed the speech of a noble messenger" (69:40). The pronoun refers to the Quran. What is understood from the context is that the "noble messenger" refers to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم. This is a confirmation of his messengership (الرسالة) in the face of their claims that he was a poet or a soothsayer.

There is no harm in attributing the Quran to his speech, for it is only attributed to him insofar as he is a messenger. A messenger (الرسول), qua messenger, brings nothing but the speech of the One who sent him. This has been clarified with the utmost clarity in His subsequent saying: **تَنْزِيلٌ مِنْ رَبِّ الْعَالَمِينَ** "a revelation from the Lord of all the worlds" (69:43).

It has been said that the "noble messenger" refers to Gabriel, but the context does not support this. Had he been intended, it would have been more appropriate to negate its being something brought down by devils, as was done in Surah al-Shu‘ara’. Furthermore, His subsequent saying: **وَلَوْ تَقَوَّلَ عَلَيْنَا بَعْضَ الْأَقَاوِيلِ** "Had he faked any sayings in Our name" (69:44) and what follows it, is only suitable if the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم is the one intended by the "noble messenger."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَمَا هُوَ بِقَوْلِ شَاعِرٍ قَلِيلًا مَا تُؤْمِنُونَ** "and it is not the speech of a poet; little is it that you believe" (69:41). This is a negation that the Quran is a composed verse authored by a poet; the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم never composed poetry nor was he a poet.

His saying: **قَلِيلًا مَا تُؤْمِنُونَ** "little is it that you believe" is a reproach to their society, as the majority of them did not believe, and none believed except a few.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَا بِقَوْلِ كَاهِنٍ قَلِيلًا مَا تَذَكَّرُونَ** "nor is it the speech of a soothsayer; little is it that you take admonition" (69:42). This is a negation that the Quran is soothsaying (الكهانة) or that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم was a soothsayer who received the Quran from the jinn who cast it to him.

His saying: **قَلِيلًا مَا تَذَكَّرُونَ** "little is it that you take admonition" is also a reproach to their society.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **تَنْزِيلٌ مِنْ رَبِّ الْعَالَمِينَ** "a revelation from the Lord of all the worlds" (69:43). That is, it is sent down from the Lord of all the worlds and is not the fabrication of the Messenger who attributed it to Allah, as previously indicated.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلَوْ تَقَوَّلَ عَلَيْنَا بَعْضَ الْأَقَاوِيلِ** — until His saying — **حَاجِزِينَ**. It is said: **تقول** (he fabricated) against so-and-so when he invents a saying from himself and attributes it to him. The **وتين** (aorta) — according to what al-Raghib mentioned — is a vein that supplies the liver, and if it is severed, its owner dies; it is also said to be the life-line of the heart.

The meaning is: "Had he fabricated against Us" — this noble messenger whom We charged with Our message and sent to you with a Quran We revealed to him — and had he invented "some sayings" and attributed them to Us, "We would have surely seized him by the right hand" (لَأَخَذْنَا مِنْهُ بِالْيَمِينِ), just as a criminal is seized by his hand. Or it means We would have severed his right hand, or it means We would have taken vengeance upon him with power (القوة), as in the narration of al-Qummi. "Then We would have surely cut off his aorta" (ثُمَّ لَقَطَعْنَا مِنْهُ الْوَتِينَ) and killed him for fabricating against Us. "And none of you could have" (فَمَا مِنْكُمْ مِنْ أَحَدٍ عَنْهُ) "shielded him" (حَاجِزِينَ) — that is, protected him from Us or saved him from Our punishment and destruction.

This is a threat to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم on the assumption that he were to forge a lie against Allah and attribute to Him something He did not say, while he is a messenger from Him whom He honored with His prophethood and chose for His message.

Thus, the verses are similar in meaning to His saying: **وَلَوْلَا أَنْ ثَبَّتْنَاكَ لَقَدْ كِدْتَ تَرْكَنُ إِلَيْهِمْ شَيْئًا قَلِيلًا إِذًا لَأَذَقْنَاكَ ضِعْفَ الْحَيَاةِ وَضِعْفَ الْمَمَاتِ ثُمَّ لَا تَجِدُ لَكَ عَلَيْنَا نَصِيرًا** "Had We not fortified you, you would have certainly inclined toward them a little. Then We would have surely made you taste a double [punishment] in this life and a double [punishment] after death, and then you would not have found for yourself any helper against Us" (17:74-75). Likewise is His saying regarding the prophets after mentioning His great blessings upon them: **وَلَوْ أَشْرَكُوا لَحَبِطَ عَنْهُمْ مَا كَانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ** "and had they ascribed any partners [to Him], what they used to do would have surely come to naught" (6:88).

Therefore, the objection does not hold that the verses imply that anyone who claims prophethood and forges lies against Allah will be destroyed and punished by Allah in this world with the severest punishment—a claim refuted by the existence of some lying claimants to prophethood.

This is because the threat in the verse is directed at the Messenger who is truthful in his message, should he fabricate against Allah and attribute to Him some things that are not from Him; it is not directed at every claimant to prophethood who is a fabricator against Allah in his very claim of prophethood and his reporting from Allah the Exalted.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِنَّهُ لَتَذْكِرَةٌ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ** "Indeed it is a reminder for the God-conscious" (69:48). It reminds them, by virtue of their godwariness (التقوى) — *the state of being conscious of Allah's presence and avoiding His displeasure*, of the realities of the Origin (المبدأ) and the Return (المعاد), and informs them of their ranks with Allah and their stations in the Hereafter and Paradise. That which possesses such a quality cannot be a fabrication or a forgery. Thus, the verse is presented as an argument for the Quran being free from fabrication and forgery.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِنَّا لَنَعْلَمُ أَنَّ مِنْكُمْ مُكَذِّبِينَ وَإِنَّهُ لَحَسْرَةٌ عَلَى الْكَافِرِينَ** "Indeed We know that there are some among you who deny [it], and indeed it will be a [cause of] regret for the faithless" (69:49-50). This will become manifest to them on the Day of Regret.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِنَّهُ لَحَقُّ الْيَقِينِ فَسَبِّحْ بِاسْمِ رَبِّكَ الْعَظِيمِ** "Indeed it is the certain truth. So celebrate the name of your Lord, the All-supreme" (69:51-52). Discussion has already preceded regarding the counterparts of these two verses at the end of Surah al-Waqi‘ah. The two Surahs are united in their objective, which is the description of the Day of Resurrection, and they are united in the context of their conclusions, which is the oath upon the reality of the Quran that foretells the Day of Resurrection. Both Surahs conclude with the fact that the Quran and what it foretells regarding the occurrence of the Inevitable Event is the certain truth (حق اليقين). This is followed by the command to glorify the Name of the Supreme Lord, who is transcendent above creating the world in vain without a Return, and above the nullification of the true knowledge that the Quran provides regarding the matter of the Origin and the Return.

— Completed, and praise be to Allah —
